content.problemStatement:
Today is an important day for Chef Hammoda as a lot of customers will be visiting his restaurant.

The restaurant contains NNN tables, and each customer will sit at one table.

Initially, Chef Hammoda plans for the dish served to the iii-th table to contain AiA_iAi​ ingredients.

However, he thinks that a group of three dishes is delicious if the sum of the number of ingredients of the three dishes is divisible by 333.

Hammoda wants every consecutive group of 333 tables to have delicious dishes. To achieve this, he can add as many ingredients as he wants to each dish.

Help him determine the **minimum** number of additional ingredients needed to make every group of three consecutive tables delicious.

More formally, solve the following problem:

You are given an array A=[A1,A2,…,AN]A = [A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N]A=[A1​,A2​,…,AN​] of length NNN. You're allowed to increment each element however much you like.

Find the **minimum** number of increments needed so that in the resulting array, the sum of every three consecutive elements is divisible by 333


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of tables.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — number of ingredients initially present in the ithi^{th}ith dish.




Output Format
For each test case, print on a new line a single integer: the minimum number of increments needed to make every length-333 subarray of AAA have a sum that's divisible by 333.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 10^5.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (N ≥ 3) – the number of tables. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN – the initial number of ingredients at each table.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum total number of ingredients that have to be added so that the sum of every three consecutive tables is divisible by 3.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: the only triple (1,2,3) already sums to 6 which is divisible by 3, so no increments are needed.\nTest case 2: one optimal choice is to add 1 ingredient to positions 4, 7 and 10 (indices are 1‑based). The total added ingredients = 3 and every triple now has sum divisible by 3.\nTest case 3: add 1 ingredient to positions 3, 5 and 7 (total 3) and additionally add 1 more ingredient to position 5 (making it +2 overall). The minimal total increments required is 4.
All elements are already multiples of 3, therefore every triple sum is 0 mod 3 and no increments are required.
Choosing increments (0,0,1,0,1) gives total cost 2 and satisfies all three‑element windows. No solution with cost 0 or 1 exists.

solution.approach:
Only the value of each element modulo 3 matters because adding 3 ingredients does not change the divisibility condition but costs 3 extra increments. Therefore each element can be increased by 0, 1 or 2 (cost equals the increment). Let inc[i] be the chosen increment for position i (0 ≤ inc[i] ≤ 2). For every i (1 ≤ i ≤ N‑2) we need:
    (A[i] + inc[i]) + (A[i+1] + inc[i+1]) + (A[i+2] + inc[i+2]) ≡ 0 (mod 3).
If inc[i] and inc[i+1] are already known, inc[i+2] is forced:
    inc[i+2] ≡ -(A[i] + inc[i] + A[i+1] + inc[i+1] + A[i+2]) (mod 3).
Thus, fixing the first two increments determines the whole array uniquely. There are only 3 × 3 = 9 possibilities for (inc[1], inc[2]). For each of them we compute the remaining inc values in O(N) and sum the costs. The answer is the minimum total cost among the nine candidates.
The algorithm runs in O(9·N) = O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (9 · N operations).
